editorsNote: Today's landscape is defined by a massive shift toward 'Agentic AI' and autonomous scientific discovery. Major players like OpenAI and Google DeepMind are moving beyond chatbots to systems that solve 80-year-old math conjectures and autonomously design new medicines, while the industry undergoes a structural reorganization of talent and capital toward these agent-first architectures.

healthcareStories.headline: Google DeepMind Unveils 'Co-Scientist' for Autonomous Drug Discovery
healthcareStories.summary:
Google DeepMind has introduced 'Co-Scientist,' a multi-agent AI system built on Gemini that autonomously generates, debates, and evolves scientific hypotheses. Published in Nature, the system is designed to act as a 'jetpack for scientists,' connecting disparate facts across massive volumes of biomedical literature to propose novel research directions. In real-world tests, the system has already demonstrated the ability to identify drug-repurposing candidates for liver fibrosis and accelerate genetic research into reversing cellular aging.

Specifically, Stanford researchers used Co-Scientist to find that the cancer drug vorinostat could block 91% of a scarring-linked response in liver cells—a connection previously buried in obscure literature. Another team at the Abudayyeh-Gootenberg Lab used the tool to slash the time required to analyze massive genetic screening datasets from six months to just a few days. The system is now being rolled out as an experimental tool for researchers via Google Labs.

Why it matters: This marks a transition from AI as a search assistant to AI as a proactive research partner. By automating the hypothesis generation and data synthesis phases, Co-Scientist could significantly shorten the decade-long drug development cycle and unlock treatments for complex diseases like ALS and metabolic liver disease.

healthcareStories.headline: Anthropic and Bristol Myers Squibb Ink Landmark 'Claude Enterprise' Deal
healthcareStories.summary:
Bristol Myers Squibb (BMS) has announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic to deploy Claude Enterprise as the 'shared intelligence platform' across its global operations. The deal will empower over 30,000 employees with agentic capabilities, moving beyond simple conversational AI to integrate Claude directly into the workflows of research, clinical development, and manufacturing. BMS aims to use 'Claude Code' to accelerate software and data science development, effectively standardizing how the company builds AI-driven capabilities.

The partnership focuses on breaking down decades of data silos that have historically trapped valuable scientific insights. By using Claude as an agentic layer, BMS plans to automate complex tasks such as connecting institutional knowledge across disconnected systems and embedding AI agents into priority drug-development workflows. This is one of the largest enterprise deployments of Anthropic's technology to date in the biopharma sector.

Why it matters: This deal signals that major pharmaceutical companies are no longer just 'experimenting' with LLMs but are now building their entire operational infrastructure around agentic AI. It highlights a shift toward 'AI-native' biopharma where agents handle the heavy lifting of data integration and software engineering.

healthcareStories.headline: AI-Designed Miniproteins Target Previously 'Undruggable' Receptors
healthcareStories.summary:
Researchers from the University of Washington's Institute for Protein Design and Skape Bio have used AI to create computationally designed miniproteins that can activate or block G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs). GPCRs are critical because they communicate with nearly every physiological process, including sensing adrenaline and insulin, yet many have remained inaccessible to conventional drug discovery due to their complex dynamic motions.

The study, published in Nature, demonstrates that AI can envision new proteins that capitalize on the specific movements of these receptors to toggle them on or off. This generalized approach allows for the creation of highly specific drug candidates for metabolic, inflammatory, and neurologic diseases. The AI-designed molecules showed the ability to block or activate receptors with a level of precision that traditional methods have struggled to achieve.

Why it matters: GPCRs are the targets of roughly one-third of all FDA-approved drugs, but many remain 'undruggable.' This AI-led breakthrough provides a repeatable framework for targeting these biologically critical receptors, potentially leading to a new generation of precision medicines for diseases that currently lack effective treatments.

techStories.headline: OpenAI Model Solves 80-Year-Old Erdős Math Conjecture
techStories.summary:
OpenAI has announced that one of its general-purpose reasoning models has autonomously produced an original proof disproving the 'planar unit distance conjecture,' a problem first posed by Paul Erdős in 1946. Unlike a previous controversial claim in late 2025, this discovery has been verified by prominent mathematicians, including Fields Medalist Tim Gowers and Thomas Bloom, who runs the official Erdős problems registry. The model achieved the breakthrough by applying deep number theory to a geometry problem in a way that human mathematicians had not previously considered.

The proof demonstrates that the model is capable of 'true' original reasoning rather than just rediscovering known information. The companion paper, published on arXiv, details how the model navigated the complex mathematical landscape to find the counterexample. This is being hailed as the first time an AI has solved a prominent open problem central to the field of mathematics without human guidance on the proof's structure.

Why it matters: Solving open conjectures is a benchmark for 'System 2' reasoning—the ability to think deeply and logically over long horizons. This success suggests that LLMs are evolving into powerful tools for formal verification and discovery in hard sciences, moving beyond pattern matching to genuine problem-solving.

techStories.headline: Meta Reorganizes 7,000 Staff into New 'AI-Native' Units
techStories.summary:
Meta Platforms has initiated a massive internal restructuring, reassigning approximately 7,000 employees into four new AI-focused organizations: Applied AI Engineering, Agent Transformation Accelerator, Central Analytics, and Enterprise Solutions. This move coincides with a 10% reduction in the overall workforce (roughly 8,000 layoffs) as the company pivots its capital and talent toward generative AI and agentic infrastructure. CEO Mark Zuckerberg noted that the company is moving into a phase where models learn by 'watching smart people do things,' necessitating a flatter, more engineering-heavy structure.

Internal memos reveal that Meta is deploying a monitoring tool called the 'Model Capability Initiative' (MCI) to track worker keystrokes and mouse movements to train internal AI models. The goal is to create 'AI-native' design structures with fewer management layers, where AI agents assist with coding, research, and internal operations. Meta's projected AI infrastructure spending for 2026 has been raised to a staggering $145 billion.

Why it matters: Meta's 'Year of Efficiency' has evolved into a 'Year of AI Transformation.' The company is effectively cannibalizing its traditional social media management layers to fund a massive bet on AI agents, signaling a broader industry trend where human labor is being redirected to train the very models that will eventually automate their previous roles.

techStories.headline: SpaceX S-1 Reveals Anthropic is Paying $1.25B Monthly for Compute
techStories.summary:
A bombshell disclosure in SpaceX's IPO filing has revealed that Anthropic has agreed to pay SpaceX $1.25 billion per month for compute access through May 2029. The total contract, valued at approximately $45 billion, secures Anthropic's access to SpaceX's 'Colossus' data center clusters in Memphis. This arrangement is highly unusual as it involves two direct competitors in the frontier AI space—Elon Musk's xAI and Anthropic—sharing infrastructure.

The deal highlights the emergence of 'compute' as a standalone strategic asset class. Analysts suggest that the scale of infrastructure required for the next generation of models is so vast that even fierce rivals are forced into 'coopetition.' Anthropic is reportedly using this capacity to power its latest Claude models and its rapidly growing enterprise agent business, which is projected to generate $10.9 billion in revenue in Q2 2026.

Why it matters: This contract value nearly matches SpaceX's entire standalone revenue from 2025, illustrating that the AI compute market is becoming as large as the aerospace and satellite industries. It also shows that the bottleneck for AI progress remains physical infrastructure, leading to unprecedented financial alliances between tech giants.

socialHighlights.content: The era of 'Auto-Regressive LLMs as the only path' is ending. The OpenAI Erdős proof is impressive, but notice it required a reasoning wrapper. We need World Models that understand physics and logic natively, not just through text tokens. The next leap isn't more parameters, it's better architecture.

Just released our 2026 Prompting Masterclass. The biggest takeaway: 'Think step-by-step' is now a legacy technique. With agentic workflows, you don't just prompt; you design an environment for the agent to iterate. We are moving from 'Prompt Engineering' to 'Agent Orchestration'.

Watching the Meta MCI (Model Capability Initiative) rollout. The 'Data Flywheel' has moved from public web data to internal 'expert' data. Companies are now essentially recording their best employees to 'distill' their expertise into agents. It's the ultimate form of knowledge transfer, but the social implications are massive.

Co-Scientist is now live in Google Labs. We're seeing it find new drug leads for ALS in hours that took labs years. AI isn't just helping us write papers; it's helping us decide what to experiment on next. The scientific method is getting an upgrade. 🧬🚀

googlePocItems.title: Building a Multi-Agent Research Assistant with Vertex AI Agent Platform
googlePocItems.description: Create a 'Co-Scientist' style POC that uses multiple Gemini 1.5 Pro agents to research a medical topic, debate findings, and generate a structured hypothesis.

guide.instruction: In the Vertex AI Agent Builder, create three distinct agents: a 'Literature Reviewer' (focused on data extraction), a 'Skeptic' (focused on finding contradictions), and a 'Synthesizer' (focused on hypothesis generation).
Connect your agents to a Vertex AI Search data store containing a corpus of medical PDFs or PubMed abstracts using the 'Search as a Tool' feature.
Use the Agent Platform's orchestration layer to pass the output of the Reviewer to the Skeptic. Instruct the Skeptic to find flaws in the Reviewer's logic before passing both to the Synthesizer.

prerequisites: Google Cloud Project with Vertex AI enabled
A small dataset of research papers (PDFs)

googlePocItems.title: Real-time Anomaly Detection in Healthcare Data with BigQuery AI
googlePocItems.description: Deploy a production-ready anomaly detection system for patient monitoring data using the new GA 'DETECT_ANOMALIES' function in BigQuery.

guide.instruction: Load your patient vitals data into a BigQuery table. Ensure you have a timestamp and the metric you want to monitor (e.g., heart rate).
Use the ML.CREATE_MODEL statement with the 'ARIMA_PLUS' type to create a baseline of normal patient vitals.
guide.codeSnippet: CREATE OR REPLACE MODEL `patient_vitals.anomaly_model` OPTIONS(model_type='ARIMA_PLUS', time_series_timestamp_col='ts', time_series_data_col='heart_rate') AS SELECT ts, heart_rate FROM `patient_vitals.historical_data`

guide.instruction: Call the ML.DETECT_ANOMALIES function on your incoming target data stream to identify spikes or drops that deviate from the historical norm.
guide.codeSnippet: SELECT * FROM ML.DETECT_ANOMALIES(MODEL `patient_vitals.anomaly_model`, STRUCT(0.95 AS anomaly_prob_threshold), TABLE `patient_vitals.new_stream`)

deepLearningSpotlight.title: The End of the 'Jobpocalypse' Narrative
deepLearningSpotlight.summary:
In the latest edition of 'The Batch,' Andrew Ng forcefully counters the prevailing narrative that AI will lead to mass unemployment. He argues that while AI is indeed automating specific tasks—particularly in software engineering—it is simultaneously creating a massive 'idea backlog' that requires more, not fewer, engineers to execute. Ng points out that the U.S. unemployment rate remains healthy at 4.3% despite the rapid adoption of coding agents. He suggests that AI labs and large corporations have a strategic incentive to exaggerate AI's displacement potential to justify high valuations or restructuring. 

Ng's perspective is that we are entering an 'AI employment boom' where the bottleneck has shifted from engineering capacity to decision-making speed. He emphasizes that the most successful organizations will be those that invest in 'human skills'—specifically judgment and product management—to decide what to build with the newfound speed AI provides. He urges learners to focus on becoming 'AI-native generalists' who can orchestrate agents rather than just writing code manually.
deepLearningSpotlight.author: Andrew Ng

deepLearningSpotlight.title: AI Mammogram Diagnosis: The Real-World Gap
deepLearningSpotlight.summary:
This research highlight examines two new studies testing Google's breast cancer detection models in clinical settings. Despite the AI system being introduced in 2020 and showing superhuman performance in lab settings, the studies reveal that it still hasn't been widely adopted for diagnosing current patients. The primary hurdles are not technical but 'real-world'—including integration with legacy hospital IT systems, regulatory liability, and the 'swivel-chair bottleneck' where doctors must manually move data between the AI tool and patient records.

The article highlights that for AI to actually save lives in healthcare, the focus must shift from 'model accuracy' to 'workflow integration.' It discusses how new 'medical transcription specialists' like Corti's Symphony are attempting to solve this by acting as an invisible layer that handles the data movement and documentation, allowing doctors to focus on the AI's insights rather than the logistics of using the tool.
deepLearningSpotlight.author: The Batch Team

generalLearningItems.title: Prompt Engineering Masterclass 2026
generalLearningItems.summary: A complete overhaul of prompting techniques, focusing on 'Deep Research' modes and iterative agent collaboration rather than single-shot phrases.

generalLearningItems.title: Fine-Tuning NVIDIA Cosmos for Robot Video Generation
generalLearningItems.summary: A technical guide on using LoRA/DoRA to fine-tune the latest NVIDIA Cosmos models for generating synthetic video data to train bipedal robots.

generalLearningItems.title: Anthropic Cookbook: Building Agentic Workflows with Claude Code
generalLearningItems.summary: New recipes for integrating 'Claude Code' into enterprise CI/CD pipelines to automate bug fixing and documentation updates.
